worth_it_explanation: Policy problems drain a Meta Ads account in two ways. A rejected ad spends nothing while its variants carry the budget, which skews the test you thought you were running, and an ad in limited delivery serves to a fraction of the audience it could reach. The checks read from Account Quality and the ad's delivery status, so they take minutes per ad set. The bigger payoff is the account itself. Repeated policy flags and an undeclared restricted-category campaign both put an account on a path toward restriction, and an account restriction stops every campaign at once. Catching the pattern early is the difference between editing one ad and rebuilding an account from a fresh asset.
intro: Meta reviews every ad against its Advertising Standards before the ad serves and again after it goes live. An ad can come back rejected, run in limited delivery, or trip a restricted-category requirement the advertiser never declared. These checks confirm that no ads are stalled in a rejected or limited state without action, that any housing, employment, or credit ads are declared under a Special Ad Category, and that the account is not accumulating policy flags that put it at risk of restriction.

audit_checks.title: Active ads are rejected or in limited delivery with no review requested
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A rejected ad serves nothing, so its share of the budget shifts to the ads that did clear review and the comparison between creatives is no longer a fair test. An ad in limited delivery reaches a slice of its eligible audience, so the ad set underdelivers without an obvious cause in the spend.

educate.base:
Meta reviews every ad against its Advertising Standards before the ad serves, looking at the image, video, text, targeting, and the destination the ad points to. An ad that fails review is marked rejected and does not serve. An ad that clears review but trips a softer policy signal can land in limited delivery, where it serves to a reduced audience rather than stopping outright.

Both states are visible in the ad's delivery column and in the Account Quality tool, where Meta names the reason and offers a path to request another review. When a review is not requested and the ad is not edited, the ad sits in that state for the life of the ad set. Inside an active ad set, that has a measurement cost. The budget Meta would have spent on the rejected ad flows to the remaining ads, so a creative test that looks like it ran across three ads actually ran across two.

A rejection is not always a content problem the advertiser agrees with. Meta's review is mostly automated, and an ad can be flagged in error. The path is the same either way. Read the stated reason, then either edit the ad to resolve it or request a review if the rejection looks incorrect.

A few states are worth separating when you read the delivery column:
  • Rejected: the ad failed review and serves nothing until edited or successfully re-reviewed
  • Limited delivery: the ad serves to a reduced audience because of a policy signal short of rejection
  • In review: a normal temporary state, usually cleared within 24 hours, that is not itself a finding
You may want to confirm that every rejected or limited ad has either been edited or had a review requested, rather than left to sit while the ad set spends around it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, rejections often trace to the product itself or to claims in the ad copy, for example a supplement ad that states a health outcome Meta's policies restrict. You may want to read the rejection reason against the specific product before assuming the whole ad set has a problem since a single product variant can be the cause while the rest of the catalog clears review.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, rejections often trace to the offer or the wording around it, for example copy that implies knowledge of a personal attribute, or a landing page Meta cannot reach to review. You may want to confirm the destination page loads for Meta's reviewer and that the form and copy avoid the personal-attribute phrasing the policies restrict.

learn_more.source_title: How to troubleshoot a rejected ad
Ads Review Policy for Businesses on Facebook and Instagram

investigate.intro: Read the delivery status of the active ads and check Account Quality for any rejected or limited-delivery ads that have not been edited or sent for review.

ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open Ads Manager and switch to the 'Ads' tab so you are looking at individual ads rather than campaigns or ad sets.
Add or read the 'Delivery' column and filter or scan for ads marked 'Rejected' or 'Limited delivery'.
For each one, hover or open the ad to read the stated policy reason, and note whether the ad sits inside an ad set that is still spending.

account_quality.label: Account Quality
steps: Open the Account Quality tool from Business Support Home for the account you are auditing.
Review the list of rejected ads and any account-level flags, and read the reason Meta provides for each rejection.
Note which rejections have an open 'Request review' option that was never used since those are ads left stalled rather than acted on.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per active ad, its delivery status and whether a rejected or limited ad has been edited or sent for review. An account where every rejected or limited ad has been edited or has a review in progress passes. An account with rejected or limited ads sitting inside spending ad sets with no edit and no review requested is a fail, recorded with the ad names and their stated reasons.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the delivery status and the action taken for each rejected or limited-delivery ad above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has active ads in a rejected or limited-delivery state that have not been edited or sent for review. A rejected ad serves nothing, so its budget shifts to the ads that cleared review and the creative comparison is no longer a fair test, while a limited-delivery ad reaches only part of its eligible audience. {{voice_recommend}} reading the stated reason for each affected ad in Account Quality, then either editing the ad to resolve the policy issue or requesting a review where the rejection looks incorrect. Leaving these ads in place lets the ad set keep spending around them without the creative the test was meant to evaluate.

audit_checks.title: Housing, employment, or credit ads are running without a Special Ad Category declared
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Running a restricted-category ad without declaring it is a policy violation that can lead to rejection or account restriction, and a restriction stops every campaign in the account, not only the undeclared one. The declaration also changes which targeting options are allowed, so an undeclared campaign may be targeting in ways the category prohibits.

educate.base:
Meta requires advertisers to declare a Special Ad Category when an ad offers housing, employment, or credit opportunities. The declaration is set at the campaign level in Ads Manager, or through the special ad category field in the Marketing API, and it changes the targeting the campaign is allowed to use. Detailed age, gender, ZIP code, and many interest and behavior options are removed, and the audience is built within a restricted set designed to prevent discrimination in these categories.

The obligation is on the advertiser to recognize when an ad falls into one of these categories and declare it. An ad promoting apartment listings, job openings, or a loan or credit-card offer belongs in the matching category. Running such an ad with the category set to 'None' is a policy violation. Meta can reject the ad, and a pattern of undeclared restricted-category ads is the kind of repeated violation that moves an account toward restriction.

The declaration also has a measurement consequence that is easy to miss. Because the allowed targeting differs, a campaign declared correctly and one left undeclared are not comparable. Historical performance from an undeclared run does not predict performance once the category is applied since the audience is built from a different set of controls.

A few ad types fall under these categories and need a declaration:
  • Housing: property listings, rentals, real-estate services, and homeowner financing
  • Employment: job openings, internships, and recruiting offers
  • Credit: credit cards, loans, vehicle financing, and similar offers
You may want to confirm that any campaign promoting one of these is set to the matching Special Ad Category rather than left at the default since the default is treated as a declaration that the ad does not offer one of these opportunities.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the categories surface less often, but financing offers do qualify. A campaign promoting a 'buy now, pay later' option, a store-branded credit card, or vehicle financing offers a credit opportunity and belongs in the credit Special Ad Category. You may want to confirm any campaign that advertises a financing or credit product is declared, even when the rest of the catalog is ordinary retail.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, these categories are central rather than incidental since recruiting, real-estate, and lending businesses run lead campaigns directly in them. You may want to confirm every campaign tied to a job listing, a property, or a loan or credit offer carries the matching Special Ad Category, and that the lead form and targeting were built within the restricted options the category allows.

learn_more.source_title: How to choose a Special Ad Category

investigate.intro: Review each campaign's Special Ad Category setting against what the ads actually promote, and flag any housing, employment, or credit ad left at the default 'None'.

ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open Ads Manager and review the campaigns, then open each campaign's settings to read its Special Ad Category value.
Read the ads and landing pages in each campaign and decide whether any offer a housing, employment, or credit opportunity.
Flag any campaign that promotes one of these but shows the category as 'None' since the offer and the declaration disagree.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign, what the ads promote and the campaign's Special Ad Category setting. A campaign whose ads do not offer a restricted opportunity, or whose category matches the opportunity it offers, passes. A campaign promoting housing, employment, or credit with the category set to 'None' is a fail, recorded with the campaign name and the category it should carry.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record what each campaign promotes and its Special Ad Category setting above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is running ads offering housing, employment, or credit opportunities without declaring the matching Special Ad Category. Meta requires this declaration for these categories, and an undeclared restricted-category ad is a policy violation that can be rejected and, as a pattern, can move the account toward restriction that would stop every campaign. The declaration also limits the targeting Meta allows, so an undeclared campaign may be targeting in ways the category prohibits. {{voice_recommend}} setting each affected campaign to the correct Special Ad Category and rebuilding its audience within the restricted targeting the category permits, rather than relying on performance from the undeclared run, which was built on targeting the category does not allow.

audit_checks.title: Repeated policy violations are pushing the account toward a restriction
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Meta weighs severe and repeated violations when deciding to restrict a business, and a restriction limits or stops the account's ability to advertise across every campaign at once. Recovering from a restriction is slower and less certain than fixing the individual ads that caused the flags, so the cost of ignoring the pattern is the whole account rather than one ad.

educate.base:
Meta's enforcement is not limited to the ad in front of the reviewer. The account itself carries a record, and Meta weighs whether a business is severely or repeatedly violating its Advertising Standards when it decides to restrict that business. A restriction limits or removes the ability to advertise, and it applies to the account, so it stops campaigns that never had a policy problem of their own.

The signal builds from individual rejections. A handful of unrelated rejections that were each edited and resolved is different from a steady stream of the same violation across many ads, or a serious violation in a category Meta treats strictly, for example circumventing the review system or a prohibited business model. The Account Quality tool is where this history lives. It shows past rejections, any active restrictions, and the standing of the account.

Reading the pattern matters more than counting rejections. Several flags pointing at the same root cause, such as a recurring claim in the copy or a destination the policies restrict, say the account will keep generating violations until that cause is fixed. Resolving the underlying issue does more for the account's standing than re-requesting review on each ad in turn.

A few signals in Account Quality are worth weighing together:
  • A cluster of rejections sharing one policy reason rather than scattered unrelated ones
  • Any active account-level restriction or warning, not only ad-level rejections
  • A serious-violation flag, which Meta treats more heavily than ordinary content rejections
You may want to treat a repeating reason as a process problem to fix at the source since clearing the underlying cause protects the account more than addressing each rejected ad on its own.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a restriction risk often builds from the catalog, where one non-compliant product type generates the same rejection across many ads and product sets. You may want to trace whether the flags concentrate on a product category since pulling or fixing that category at the source can stop the pattern that is putting the whole account at risk.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, the risk often builds from the offer or the landing experience repeated across campaigns, for example a claim in the copy or a destination the policies restrict that appears in every variant. You may want to confirm whether the flags trace to one reusable asset since fixing that asset clears the source rather than the symptoms.

learn_more.source_title: About the enforcement of Meta Advertising Standards

investigate.intro: Read the account's history in Account Quality for clustered rejections, serious-violation flags, and any active restriction, and trace recurring flags to their shared cause.

account_quality.label: Account Quality
steps: Open the Account Quality tool from Business Support Home for the account you are auditing.
Review the history of rejected ads and any account-level restrictions or warnings, and read the policy reason attached to each.
Group the rejections by reason and note whether several share one cause, and whether any flag is marked as a serious violation rather than ordinary content.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the account's standing in Account Quality, the count and grouping of recent rejections, and whether any restriction or serious-violation flag is present. An account with only scattered, resolved rejections and no restriction passes. An account with a cluster of rejections sharing one cause, a serious-violation flag, or an active restriction is a fail, recorded with the shared reason and the standing shown.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the account standing, the grouping of recent rejections, and any restriction shown in Account Quality above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s account is accumulating policy violations in a pattern that puts it at risk of restriction. Meta weighs severe and repeated violations when it restricts a business, and a restriction applies to the whole account, so it would stop campaigns that never had a policy problem of their own. The rejections appear to share a cause rather than being scattered and unrelated. {{voice_recommend}} tracing the recurring reason to its source, whether that is a claim in the copy, a product type, or a destination the policies restrict, and resolving it there rather than re-requesting review on each ad. Clearing the underlying cause protects the account's standing more than addressing the rejected ads one at a time.
